event: The first human settlers arrive in the area of present-day Finland as the ice sheet retreats after the last Ice Age.
Comb Ceramic pottery culture spreads across Finland, marking a flourishing Stone Age hunter-gatherer society.
Bronze Age influences from Scandinavia and the east reach coastal and inland Finland.
The Iron Age begins in Finland, bringing new tools, agriculture, and growing permanent settlements.
The historian Tacitus mentions the 'Fenni' in his work Germania, one of the earliest references to peoples of the region.
Viking Age trade routes link Finnish tribes with Scandinavia, the Baltic, and the rivers leading east.
According to legend, Sweden's King Eric IX leads the First Swedish Crusade into Finland, beginning Christianization.
The Second Swedish Crusade consolidates Swedish control over the Tavastia region of Finland.
The Third Swedish Crusade extends Swedish rule into Karelia and Vyborg Castle is founded.
The Treaty of Noteborg fixes the first border between Sweden and Novgorod, dividing Finnish lands.
Finland gains the right to take part in electing the Swedish king, recognizing it as an integral part of the realm.
Finland becomes part of the Kalmar Union joining Sweden with Denmark and Norway under one monarch.
Sweden leaves the Kalmar Union under Gustav Vasa, and Finland remains a Swedish province.
Mikael Agricola publishes the first Finnish-language primer, laying the foundations of written Finnish.
Mikael Agricola completes the Finnish translation of the New Testament, a landmark for the Finnish language.
King Gustav Vasa founds the city of Helsinki to compete with Tallinn for Baltic trade.
The Royal Academy of Turku, Finland's first university, is established.
A catastrophic famine kills roughly a third of Finland's population during the 'Great Hunger Years'.
Russian forces occupy Finland during the Great Northern War, a period known as the 'Greater Wrath'.
The Treaty of Nystad ends the Great Northern War, ceding parts of southeastern Finland to Russia.
Russia conquers Finland from Sweden, and Finland becomes an autonomous Grand Duchy under the Russian Tsar.
Helsinki is designated the new capital of the Grand Duchy of Finland, replacing Turku.
Elias Lonnrot publishes the Kalevala, the Finnish national epic compiled from folk poetry.
Finland gains its own currency, the markka, strengthening its distinct economic identity.
Finnish is granted official status alongside Swedish following Tsar Alexander II's language decree.
Finland adopts universal and equal suffrage, becoming the first country to grant women full political rights including the right to stand for election.
Finland declares independence from Russia on 6 December amid the Russian Revolution.
A bitter civil war between Reds and Whites ends with a White victory backed by Germany.
Finland adopts a republican constitution and K. J. Stahlberg becomes its first president.
The League of Nations confirms the Aland Islands as part of Finland with autonomous and demilitarized status.
The Soviet Union invades Finland, starting the Winter War of fierce Finnish resistance.
The Moscow Peace Treaty ends the Winter War, with Finland ceding Karelia and other territories to the USSR.
Finland joins the Continuation War against the Soviet Union alongside Germany to reclaim lost lands.
Finland signs the Moscow Armistice with the USSR and turns to expel German troops in the Lapland War.
The Paris Peace Treaty formalizes Finland's borders and obligations after the Second World War.
Finland signs the Agreement of Friendship, Cooperation, and Mutual Assistance with the Soviet Union.
Finland hosts the Summer Olympic Games in Helsinki and finishes paying its war reparations to the USSR.
Finland joins the United Nations and the Nordic Council.
Helsinki hosts the Conference on Security and Cooperation in Europe, producing the landmark Helsinki Accords.
Finland becomes a full member of the European Free Trade Association (EFTA).
Finland joins the European Union following a national referendum.
Finland adopts the euro as its currency, among the first wave of eurozone members.
Tarja Halonen becomes Finland's first female president.
Nokia reaches its peak as the world's largest mobile phone maker, symbolizing Finland's tech-driven economy.
Finland celebrates the centenary of its independence.
Finland joins NATO, abandoning decades of military non-alignment after Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
